[33mdiff --git a/.cursor/skills/materialize-pr-review/SKILL.md b/.cursor/skills/materialize-pr-review/SKILL.md[m
[33mnew file mode 100644[m
[33mindex 0000000000..72045cd430[m
[33m--- /dev/null[m
[33m+++ b/.cursor/skills/materialize-pr-review/SKILL.md[m
[35m@@ -0,0 +1,148 @@[m
[32m+[m[32m---[m
[32m+[m[32mname: materialize-pr-review[m
[32m+[m[32mdescription: Perform local PR review on Materialize by inspecting the git diff and running style/tool checks locally. Use when reviewing changes locally, doing a code review on Materialize code, or when the user asks for a PR review against Materialize standards.[m
[32m+[m[32m---[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# Materialize PR Review (local)[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mPerform a **local** review: inspect the git diff and run style/tool checks yourself. Do not rely on CI; run the commands below and report what you find.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## How to perform the review[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m1. **Inspect the diff.** Use the staged diff (&#96;git diff --staged&#96;) or the diff against main (&#96;git diff main&#96; / &#96;git diff origin/main&#96;) to see what changed. Review only the changed files and lines.[m
[32m+[m[32m2. **Run local checks** (from the repo root). Run these and report failures; suggest fixes where applicable.[m
[32m+[m[32m   - **Format:** &#96;cargo fmt --check&#96; — if it fails, suggest running &#96;cargo fmt&#96;.[m
[32m+[m[32m   - **Clippy:** &#96;cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets&#96; (or scope to affected crates if the workspace is slow).[m
[32m+[m[32m   - **Lint:** &#96;bin/lint&#96; — catches general formatting nits, trailing whitespace, etc.[m
[32m+[m[32m   - **Tests (optional):** If the diff touches code paths you want to verify, run &#96;bin/cargo-test&#96; or tests for the affected crate(s) (e.g. &#96;cargo test -p mz-sql&#96;). Report any failures.[m
[32m+[m[32m3. **Apply the checklists below** to the diff: tests, code style, architecture, polish, release notes.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mWhen reviewing a Materialize PR, apply the standards in CONTRIBUTING.md, doc/developer/guide-changes.md, doc/developer/guide-testing.md, and doc/developer/guide.md.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## Review mindset[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Review the code, not the author.** Explain the *why* behind suggestions.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Err on more feedback** rather than less, even if it slows things short-term.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Push for documentation:** if something is unclear and you need an explanation, the code should be documented or rewritten—not only explained in the PR.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Favor approving** once the PR clearly improves the codebase, even if it isn’t perfect. Aim for continuous improvement, not perfection on every diff.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mUse **nit:** for preferences where reasonable people could disagree; reserve blocking comments for real issues.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## Pre-merge checklist[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mVerify before approving:[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m- [ ] **One semantic change per review unit.** The PR (or each commit, if semantic commits) does one thing. No “do X and do Y and do Z”; no bugfix + feature in the same RU. If it spans multiple CODEOWNERS areas (e.g. sql-parser + sql planner), consider asking for a split.[m
[32m+[m[32m- [ ] **Adequate testing.** Every behavior change has at least one new or modified test. No behavior change with zero test file changes. Prefer observable behavior (sqllogictest, testdrive) over brittle unit tests of stateful internals; see guide-testing.md.[m
[32m+[m[32m- [ ] **Release notes.** Any user-visible change to stable APIs (SQL syntax/semantics, source/sink behavior, documented CLI flags) has a release note in the PR description (imperative, “This release will…”, link to docs). Experimental/unstable API changes do *not* get release notes until stabilization.[m
[32m+[m[32m- [ ] **Documentation.** User-facing features have or coordinate on user docs (A-docs). Code has enough docs that a reviewer could read a short precis before reading the code.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## Change description and polish[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Description:** First line is a short imperative summary (~72 chars). Body explains rationale, alternatives, future work; optional links to issues/benchmarks. “Fix bug” with no context is insufficient.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Polish:** No &#96;// XXX&#96;, commented-out code, or stray &#96;println!&#96;/debug code. No obvious dead code or unrelated blank-line churn.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Size:** Under ~500 lines is easier to review; over ~1000 often warrants a request to split. Exceptions: generated code, large declarative tests (slt/td), or prose-only docs. If in doubt, smaller PRs are better.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**What to look for (polish):**[m
[32m+[m[32m- Leftover TODOs, &#96;// FIXME&#96;, &#96;dbg!&#96;, or &#96;println!&#96;.[m
[32m+[m[32m- Commented-out blocks that should be removed.[m
[32m+[m[32m- Unrelated formatting or blank-line changes in untouched code.[m
[32m+[m[32m- New public items (functions, types, modules) without doc comments.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## Software engineering principles[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mCheck that the change aligns with guide-changes.md “Software engineering principles.” See **What to look for: Architecture and design** and **What to look for: Code style** for concrete checkpoints. In short:[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Simplicity:** No incidental complexity; simplify redundant logic.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Avoid special casing:** Prefer composable design over extra booleans/branches.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Encapsulation:** Right crate/module (e.g. sql-parser = grammar only; sql = semantics).[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Dependencies:** Justified, preferably well-maintained.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Documentation:** Public behavior and non-obvious design documented so reviewers can judge without reading every line.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## What to look for: Tests[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Coverage**[m
[32m+[m[32m- Every behavior change has at least one new or modified test. No change to SQL semantics, sources, sinks, or CLI behavior with zero test file changes.[m
[32m+[m[32m- New SQL/query behavior: look for sqllogictest (&#96;.slt&#96;) in &#96;test/sqllogictest/&#96; or testdrive (&#96;.td&#96;).[m
[32m+[m[32m- New or changed data types / wire behavior: look for testdrive or pgtest to cover pgwire serialization.[m
[32m+[m[32m- Pure, stable logic (e.g. decoding, pure functions): unit/integration tests in crate &#96;tests/&#96; or &#96;mod tests&#96; are appropriate.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Test style (guide-testing.md, style.md)**[m
[32m+[m[32m- Prefer testing **observable behavior** (SQL results, wire protocol) over implementation details; over-testing internals makes refactors painful.[m
[32m+[m[32m- Rust tests: use &#96;#[mz_ore::test]&#96; (or &#96;#[mz_ore::test(tokio::test)]&#96; for async) so logging is initialized; prefer panics over &#96;Result&#96; in tests for better backtraces.[m
[32m+[m[32m- sqllogictest: prefer Materialize-specific &#96;.slt&#96; in &#96;test/sqllogictest/&#96;; avoid modifying upstream SQLite/CockroachDB test files.[m
[32m+[m[32m- testdrive: &#96;.td&#96; files; copyright header, no trailing spaces/newlines — run &#96;bin/lint&#96; to check.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Red flags**[m
[32m+[m[32m- Behavior change with no test changes.[m
[32m+[m[32m- New unit tests that mock many stateful dependencies for mid-stack code; suggest system tests instead.[m
[32m+[m[32m- Tests that assert on internal structure (e.g. exact plan shape) unless that’s the feature under test; prefer outcome-based assertions.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m---[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## What to look for: Code style[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Rust (style.md, guide.md)**[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Run locally and report:** &#96;cargo fmt --check&#96;, &#96;cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets&#96;, &#96;bin/lint&#96;. If any fail, note the failures and suggest fixes (e.g. run &#96;cargo fmt&#96;, address clippy lints).[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Imports:** Group order &#96;std&#96; → external crates (including &#96;mz_*&#96;) → &#96;crate&#96;; one &#96;use&#96; per module; &#96;crate::&#96; not &#96;super::&#96; for crate-local imports.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Errors:** Structured errors with &#96;thiserror&#96;; no bare &#96;anyhow!("...")&#96;. &#96;Display&#96; should not print the full error chain; use &#96;ResultExt&#96;/&#96;ErrorExt&#96; (e.g. &#96;display_with_causes&#96;) when logging or surfacing to users.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Async/tasks:** Use &#96;ore::task::spawn&#96; / &#96;spawn_blocking&#96; (or &#96;RuntimeExt::spawn_named&#96; / &#96;spawn_blocking_named&#96;) instead of raw &#96;tokio::spawn&#96; so task names show in tokio-console.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Tests:** &#96;#[mz_ore::test]&#96;; panic in tests rather than returning &#96;Result&#96;.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**SQL (style.md)**[m
[32m+[m[32m- Keywords capitalized (&#96;SELECT&#96;, &#96;FROM&#96;, &#96;WHERE&#96;, &#96;CREATE TABLE&#96;); identifiers and function names lowercase (&#96;integer&#96;, &#96;varchar&#96;, &#96;coalesce&#96;). Type constructors like &#96;DATE '...'&#96; capitalize the type.[m
[32m+[m[32m- Spacing: no space between function name and &#96;(&#96;; space after keywords before &#96;(&#96;; spaces around operators; space after commas.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Error messages (style.md)**[m
[32m+[m[32m- Primary: short, factual, lowercase first letter, no trailing punctuation.[m
[32m+[m[32m- Detail/hint: complete sentences, capitalized, period; hint actionable (e.g. “Try using CAST in the CREATE SOURCE statement”).[m
[32m+[m[32m- No formatting (e.g. newlines) in message text; use quotes for identifiers/file names; avoid “unable”, “bad”, “illegal”, “unknown”; say what kind of object (e.g. “table”) when naming it.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Logging (style.md)**[m
[32m+[m[32m- Use &#96;tracing&#96; macros; sentence fragment style (no leading cap, no period).[m
[32m+[m[32m- Prefer structured fields for searchable values: &#96;info!(shard_id = ?id, "message")&#96;.[m
[32m+[m[32m- Level semantics: &#96;ERROR&#96; = data loss/corruption/invariant violation (sparingly; consider &#96;panic!&#96;); &#96;WARN&#96; = recoverable or uncertain; &#96;INFO&#96; = normal status changes; &#96;DEBUG&#96;/&#96;TRACE&#96; for noisier detail.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**System catalog / SQL functions (style.md)**[m
[32m+[m[32m- New catalog relations: normalized schema, &#96;interval&#96; for durations (not &#96;*_ms&#96; except nanosecond), kebab-case enum values, &#96;_at&#96; for timestamps, no &#96;is_&#96; prefix on booleans, pluralized relation names (e.g. &#96;mz_sources&#96;).[m
[32m+[m[32m- Internal-only functions: &#96;mz_&#96; prefix; match PostgreSQL names/args when present.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m---[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## What to look for: Architecture and design[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Simplicity (guide-changes.md)**[m
[32m+[m[32m- Code is as simple as it can be; no incidental complexity. Redundant conditionals (e.g. &#96;if x { true } else { false }&#96; → &#96;x&#96;) should be simplified.[m
[32m+[m[32m- “Getting tests to pass is half the battle”; ask whether the change could be simpler or better documented.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Special casing**[m
[32m+[m[32m- No proliferation of boolean/option parameters or feature-specific branches that could be a composable design. If the interface is growing many “if type X then…” branches, suggest a cleaner abstraction.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Encapsulation**[m
[32m+[m[32m- Logic lives in the right crate/module. **sql-parser:** grammar only; no semantic or policy validation (e.g. no rejecting &#96;LIMIT 'foo'&#96; in the parser—that belongs in &#96;sql&#96; with coercion). **sql:** planning, coercion, catalog semantics.[m
[32m+[m[32m- New code in “the easy place” is often wrong; check whether a new module or different crate would give clearer boundaries.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Dependencies**[m
[32m+[m[32m- New crates must be justified; prefer well-maintained (e.g. Tokio, reqwest). Unmaintained deps often become team-maintained forks (e.g. rust-rdkafka, rust-prometheus).[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m**Subsystem-specific (guide-changes.md “Mentoring”)**[m
[32m+[m[32m- **dataflow:** allocation-sensitive hot loops; avoid unnecessary allocations.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **sql:** less allocation-sensitive; clarity and correctness first.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **coord / persist / compute:** complex, stateful; extra care for correctness and clarity; called out in CONTRIBUTING as sensitive for external contributors.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m---[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## External contributors (CONTRIBUTING.md)[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Landing large changes:** Requires coordination with technical writers for user docs and a release note in doc/user/release-notes.md.[m
[32m+[m[32m- **Sensitive areas:** Coordinator, Persist, Compute are called out as less suited for external contributions; extra care in review there.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m## Summary for the author[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mWhen leaving the review:[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m1. **Blocking:** Must fix before merge (correctness, missing tests for behavior changes, missing release notes for user-visible changes, serious simplicity/encapsulation violations).[m
[32m+[m[32m2. **Strong suggestions:** Should fix unless there’s a good reason (e.g. docs, clarity, test quality).[m
[32m+[m[32m3. **nits:** Optional improvements.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mIf the PR improves overall codebase health and blocking items are addressed, approve even if nits remain.[m

[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/content/self-managed-deployments/configuration-system-parameters.md b/doc/user/content/self-managed-deployments/configuration-system-parameters.md[m
[33mindex 2395e614b8..76ce1daac5 100644[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/content/self-managed-deployments/configuration-system-parameters.md[m
[33m+++ b/doc/user/content/self-managed-deployments/configuration-system-parameters.md[m
[35m@@ -7,6 +7,7 @@[m [mmenu:[m
   main:[m
     parent: "sm-deployments"[m
     weight: 71[m
[32m+[m[32mdraft: true[m
 ---[m
 [m
 This guide explains how to configure system parameters for your Materialize[m
[35m@@ -20,27 +21,22 @@[m [mconnection limits, cluster replica sizes, and other operational settings.[m
 [m
 There are two ways to configure system parameters:[m
 [m
[31m-- **Using SQL**: Connect to your Materialize instance and use the [&#96;ALTER SYSTEM[m
[31m-  SET&#96;](/sql/alter-system-set/) command to modify parameters dynamically. This[m
[31m-  is useful for one-off changes or testing.[m
[32m+[m[32m1. **Using SQL**: Connect to your Materialize instance and use the[m
[32m+[m[32m   [&#96;ALTER SYSTEM SET&#96;](/sql/alter-system-set/) command to modify parameters[m
[32m+[m[32m   dynamically. This is useful for one-off changes or testing.[m
 [m
[31m-- **Using a ConfigMap**: Create a Kubernetes ConfigMap containing the parameters[m
[31m-  in JSON format and reference it in your Materialize custom resource. This is[m
[31m-  the recommended approach for persistent configuration that survives restarts[m
[31m-  and upgrades.[m
[32m+[m[32m2. **Using a ConfigMap**: Create a Kubernetes ConfigMap containing the[m
[32m+[m[32m   parameters in JSON format and reference it in your Materialize custom[m
[32m+[m[32m   resource. This is the recommended approach for persistent configuration[m
[32m+[m[32m   that survives restarts and upgrades.[m
 [m
 This guide focuses on the ConfigMap approach for self-managed deployments.[m
 [m
[31m-{{&lt; public-preview /&gt;}}[m
[32m+[m[32m## Creating a System Parameters ConfigMap[m
 [m
[31m-## Configure System Parameters via ConfigMap[m
[31m-[m
[31m-### Step 1: Create a System Parameters ConfigMap[m
[31m-[m
[31m-In the same namespace as your Materialize environment, create a[m
[31m-ConfigMap that includes a key named &#96;system-params.json&#96;. Set[m
[31m-&#96;system-params.json&#96; to a valid JSON object containing your desired system[m
[31m-parameters.[m
[32m+[m[32mCreate a ConfigMap in the same namespace as your Materialize environment with[m
[32m+[m[32myour desired system parameters. The ConfigMap must contain a key named[m
[32m+[m[32m&#96;system-params.json&#96; with a valid JSON object containing the system parameters.[m
 [m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;yaml[m
 apiVersion: v1[m
[35m@@ -62,12 +58,12 @@[m [mApply the ConfigMap to your cluster:[m
 kubectl apply -f system-params-configmap.yaml[m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 [m
[31m-### Step 2: Configure the Materialize Custom Resource[m
[32m+[m[32m## Configuring the Materialize Custom Resource[m
 [m
 Reference the ConfigMap in your Materialize custom resource by setting the[m
[31m-&#96;systemParameterConfigmapName&#96; field to the name of your ConfigMap:[m
[32m+[m[32m&#96;systemParameterConfigmapName&#96; field:[m
 [m
[31m-&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml {hl_lines="9"}[m
[32m+[m[32m&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml[m
 apiVersion: materialize.cloud/v1alpha1[m
 kind: Materialize[m
 metadata:[m
[35m@@ -85,21 +81,19 @@[m [mApply the updated Materialize resource:[m
 kubectl apply -f materialize.yaml[m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 [m
[31m-## Updating ConfigMap System Parameters[m
[32m+[m[32m## Updating System Parameters[m
 [m
[31m-To update system parameters defined in your ConfigMap, you can either:[m
[32m+[m[32mTo update system parameters, modify the ConfigMap and apply the changes:[m
 [m
[31m-- Use &#96;kubectl edit configmap&#96; to edit the ConfigMap and apply the changes:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;shell[m
[31m-  kubectl edit configmap mz-system-params -n materialize-environment[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[32m+[m[32m&#96;&#96;&#96;shell[m
[32m+[m[32mkubectl edit configmap mz-system-params -n materialize-environment[m
[32m+[m[32m&#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 [m
[31m-- Or, edit the ConfigMap YAML file and reapply:[m
[32m+[m[32mOr update the ConfigMap YAML file and reapply:[m
 [m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;shell[m
[31m-  kubectl apply -f system-params-configmap.yaml[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[32m+[m[32m&#96;&#96;&#96;shell[m
[32m+[m[32mkubectl apply -f system-params-configmap.yaml[m
[32m+[m[32m&#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 [m
 ### ConfigMap sync behavior[m
 [m
[35m@@ -121,9 +115,8 @@[m [mkubectl annotate materialize &lt;instance-name&gt; \[m
   --overwrite[m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 [m
[31m-Alternatively, you can add the &#96;configmap-reload-trigger&#96; annotation to your[m
[31m-Materialize custom resource YAML and update it whenever you need to force a[m
[31m-ConfigMap reload:[m
[32m+[m[32mAlternatively, you can add this annotation to your Materialize custom resource[m
[32m+[m[32mYAML and update it whenever you need to force a ConfigMap reload:[m
 [m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;yaml[m
 apiVersion: materialize.cloud/v1alpha1[m
[35m@@ -137,10 +130,8 @@[m [mspec:[m
   # ... rest of spec[m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 [m
[31m-{{&lt; note &gt;}}[m
[31m-Even after the ConfigMap is synced, some parameters may require a restart to[m
[31m-take effect.[m
[31m-{{&lt; /note &gt;}}[m
[32m+[m[32mNote that some parameters may require a restart to take effect even after the[m
[32m+[m[32mConfigMap is synced.[m
 [m
 ## Available System Parameters[m
 [m
[35m@@ -166,9 +157,9 @@[m [mdocumentation, or run the following SQL command in your Materialize instance:[m
 SHOW ALL;[m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 [m

 [m

[32m+[m[32mThe following example shows how to configure the maximum number of connections:[m
 [m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;yaml[m
 apiVersion: v1[m
[35m@@ -183,27 +174,9 @@[m [mdata:[m
     }[m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 [m
[31m-### Sample ConfigMap: Configuring Allowed Cluster Sizes[m
[31m-[m
[31m-The following sample ConfigMap YAML sets the &#96;allowed_cluster_replica_sizes&#96; parameter:[m
[31m-[m

[31m-  name: mz-system-params[m

[31m-data:[m
[31m-  system-params.json: |[m
[31m-    {[m
[31m-      "allowed_cluster_replica_sizes": "'25cc', '50cc', '100cc', '200cc'"[m
[31m-    }[m
[31m-&#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m

[32m+[m[32m## Example: Configuring Allowed Cluster Sizes[m
 [m

[31m-and the &#96;allowed_cluster_replica_sizes&#96; parameter:[m
[32m+[m[32mThe following example shows how to restrict the available cluster replica sizes:[m
 [m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;yaml[m
 apiVersion: v1[m
[35m@@ -214,7 +187,6 @@[m [mmetadata:[m
 data:[m

[33mindex d5704416a9..7776aa098c 100644[m

 [m
[31m-## Cancelling the Upgrade[m
[31m-[m
[31m-You may want to cancel an in-progress rollout if the upgrade has failed. This may be indicated by new pods not being healthy. Before cancelling, verify that the upgrade has not already completed by checking that the deploy generation (found via &#96;status.activeGeneration&#96;) is still the one from before the upgrade. Once an upgrade has already happened, you cannot revert using this method.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-To cancel an in-progress rollout and revert to the last completed rollout state, first retrieve the last rollout request ID from your Materialize CR:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-&#96;&#96;&#96;shell[m
[31m-kubectl get materialize &lt;instance-name&gt; -n materialize-environment -o jsonpath='{.status.lastCompletedRolloutRequest}'[m
[31m-&#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-Then, set the &#96;requestRollout&#96; back to this value:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-&#96;&#96;&#96;shell[m
[31m-kubectl patch materialize &lt;instance-name&gt; \[m
[31m-  -n materialize-environment \[m
[31m-  --type='merge' \[m
[31m-  -p "{\"spec\": {\"requestRollout\": \"&lt;lastCompletedRolloutRequest-value&gt;\"}}"[m
[31m-&#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
 ## Version Specific Upgrade Notes[m
 [m
 ### Upgrading to &#96;v26.1&#96; and later versions[m

[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/content/sql/explain-plan-operators.md b/doc/user/content/sql/explain-plan-operators.md[m
[33mdeleted file mode 100644[m
[33mindex 7c4a2bff48..0000000000[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/content/sql/explain-plan-operators.md[m
[33m+++ /dev/null[m
[35m@@ -1,52 +0,0 @@[m
[31m----[m
[31m-title: "Explain plan operators"[m
[31m-description: ""[m
[31m-menu:[m
[31m-  main:[m
[31m-    parent: reference[m
[31m-    name: Explain plan operators[m
[31m-    identifier: 'explain-plan-operators'[m
[31m-    weight: 140[m
[31m-disable_list: true[m
[31m----[m
[31m-[m
[31m-Materialize offers several output formats for [&#96;EXPLAIN[m
[31m-PLAN&#96;](/sql/explain-plan/) and debugging. LIR plans as rendered in[m
[31m-[&#96;mz_introspection.mz_lir_mapping&#96;](../../sql/system-catalog/mz_introspection/#mz_lir_mapping)[m
[31m-are deliberately succinct, while the plans in other formats give more detail.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-The decorrelated and optimized plans from &#96;EXPLAIN DECORRELATED PLAN[m
[31m-FOR ...&#96;, &#96;EXPLAIN LOCALLY OPTIMIZED PLAN FOR ...&#96;, and &#96;EXPLAIN[m
[31m-OPTIMIZED PLAN FOR ...&#96; are in a mid-level representation that is[m
[31m-closer to LIR than SQL. The raw plans from &#96;EXPLAIN RAW PLAN FOR ...&#96;[m
[31m-are closer to SQL (and therefore less indicative of how the query will[m
[31m-actually run).[m
[31m-[m
[31m-{{&lt; tabs &gt;}}[m
[31m-[m
[31m-{{&lt; tab "In fully optimized physical (LIR) plans (Default)" &gt;}}[m
[31m-{{&lt; explain-plans/operator-table data="explain_plan_operators" planType="LIR" &gt;}}[m
[31m-{{&lt; /tab &gt;}}[m
[31m-[m
[31m-{{&lt; tab "In decorrelated and optimized plans" &gt;}}[m
[31m-{{&lt; explain-plans/operator-table data="explain_plan_operators" planType="optimized" &gt;}}[m
[31m-{{&lt; /tab &gt;}}[m
[31m-[m
[31m-{{&lt; tab "In raw plans" &gt;}}[m
[31m-{{&lt; explain-plans/operator-table data="explain_plan_operators" planType="raw" &gt;}}[m
[31m-{{&lt; /tab &gt;}}[m
[31m-[m
[31m-{{&lt; /tabs &gt;}}[m
[31m-[m
[31m-Operators are sometimes marked as &#96;Fused ...&#96;. This indicates that the operator is fused with its input, i.e., the operator below it. That is, if you see a &#96;Fused X&#96; operator above a &#96;Y&#96; operator:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-&#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-→Fused X[m
[31m-  →Y[m
[31m-&#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-Then the &#96;X&#96; and &#96;Y&#96; operators will be combined into a single, more efficient operator.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-See also:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- [&#96;EXPLAIN PLAn&#96;](/sql/explain-plan/)[m
[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/content/sql/explain-plan.md b/doc/user/content/sql/explain-plan.md[m
[33mindex 370a8f70d2..7c627933cd 100644[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/content/sql/explain-plan.md[m
[33m+++ b/doc/user/content/sql/explain-plan.md[m
[35m@@ -145,8 +145,8 @@[m [mPlan Stage | Description[m
 **RAW PLAN** | Display the raw plan; this is closest to the original SQL.[m
 **DECORRELATED PLAN** | Display the decorrelated but not-yet-optimized plan.[m
 **LOCALLY OPTIMIZED** | Display the locally optimized plan (before view inlining and access path selection). This is the final stage for regular &#96;CREATE VIEW&#96; optimization.[m
[31m-**OPTIMIZED PLAN** | _(Default)_ Display the optimized plan.[m
[31m-**PHYSICAL PLAN** |  Display the physical plan; this corresponds to the operators shown in [&#96;mz_introspection.mz_lir_mapping&#96;](../../sql/system-catalog/mz_introspection/#mz_lir_mapping).[m
[32m+[m[32m**OPTIMIZED PLAN** | Display the optimized plan.[m
[32m+[m[32m**PHYSICAL PLAN** | _(Default)_ Display the physical plan; this corresponds to the operators shown in [&#96;mz_introspection.mz_lir_mapping&#96;](../../sql/system-catalog/mz_introspection/#mz_lir_mapping).[m
 [m
 ### Output modifiers[m
 [m
[35m@@ -295,7 +295,7 @@[m [mMany operators need to refer to columns in their input. These are displayed like[m
 &#96;#3&#96; for column number 3. (Columns are numbered starting from column 0). To get a better sense of[m
 columns assigned to &#96;Map&#96; operators, it might be useful to request [the &#96;arity&#96; output modifier](#output-modifiers).[m
 [m
[31m-Each operator can also be annotated with additional metadata. Some details are shown in the &#96;EXPLAIN&#96; output (&#96;EXPLAIN PHYSICAL PLAN AS TEXT&#96;), but are hidden elsewhere. &lt;a[m
[32m+[m[32mEach operator can also be annotated with additional metadata. Some details are shown in the default &#96;EXPLAIN&#96; output (&#96;EXPLAIN PHYSICAL PLAN AS TEXT&#96;), but are hidden elsewhere. &lt;a[m
 name="explain-with-join-implementations"&gt;&lt;/a&gt;In &#96;EXPLAIN OPTIMIZED[m
 PLAN&#96;, details about the implementation in the &#96;Join&#96; operator can be requested[m
 with [the &#96;join implementations&#96; output modifier](#output-modifiers) (that is,[m
[35m@@ -361,11 +361,11 @@[m [mactually run).[m
 [m
 {{&lt; tabs &gt;}}[m
 [m
[31m-{{&lt; tab "In fully optimized physical (LIR) plans (Default)" &gt;}}[m
[32m+[m[32m{{&lt; tab "In fully optimized physical (LIR) plans" &gt;}}[m
 {{&lt; explain-plans/operator-table data="explain_plan_operators" planType="LIR" &gt;}}[m
 {{&lt; /tab &gt;}}[m
 [m
[31m-{{&lt; tab "In decorrelated and optimized plans" &gt;}}[m
[32m+[m[32m{{&lt; tab "In decorrelated and optimized plans (default EXPLAIN)" &gt;}}[m
 {{&lt; explain-plans/operator-table data="explain_plan_operators" planType="optimized" &gt;}}[m
 {{&lt; /tab &gt;}}[m
 [m
[35m@@ -375,7 +375,7 @@[m [mactually run).[m
 [m
 {{&lt; /tabs &gt;}}[m
 [m
[31m-Operators are sometimes marked as &#96;Fused ...&#96;. This indicates that the operator is fused with its input, i.e., the operator below it. That is, if you see a &#96;Fused X&#96; operator above a &#96;Y&#96; operator:[m
[32m+[m[32mOperators are sometimes marked as &#96;Fused ...&#96;. We write this to mean that the operator is fused with its input, i.e., the operator below it. That is, if you see a &#96;Fused X&#96; operator above a &#96;Y&#96; operator:[m
 [m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 →Fused X[m
[35m@@ -530,4 +530,4 @@[m [mThe [&#96;EXPLAIN ANALYZE&#96;](/sql/explain-analyze/) statement will let you debug memo[m
 [m
 The privileges required to execute this statement are:[m
 [m
[31m-{{% include-headless "/headless/sql-command-privileges/explain-plan" %}}[m
[32m+[m[32m{{&lt; include-md file="shared-content/sql-command-privileges/explain-plan.md" &gt;}}[m

[33mrename to doc/user/data/index_usage_key_quantity.yml[m
[33mindex f0366e5211..8529420719 100644[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/data/examples/index_usage/index_usage_key_quantity.yml[m
[33m+++ b/doc/user/data/index_usage_key_quantity.yml[m
[35m@@ -1,59 +1,64 @@[m
 # CREATE INDEX idx_orders_view_qty on orders_view (quantity);[m
[31m-columns:[m
[31m-  - column: query[m
[31m-    header: Query[m
[31m-  - column: index_usage[m
[31m-    header: Index Usage[m
[31m-rows:[m
[32m+[m[32mqueries:[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       SELECT * FROM orders_view;[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
     index_usage: Index scan.[m
[32m+[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       SELECT * FROM orders_view WHERE quantity = 10;[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
     index_usage: Point lookup.[m
[32m+[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       SELECT * FROM orders_view WHERE quantity IN (10, 20);[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
     index_usage: Point lookup.[m
[32m+[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       SELECT * FROM orders_view WHERE quantity = 10 OR quantity = 20;[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
     index_usage: |[m
       Point lookup. Query uses &#96;OR&#96; to combine conditions on the **same** field.[m
[32m+[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       SELECT * FROM orders_view WHERE quantity = 10 AND price = 5.00;[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
     index_usage: |[m
       Point lookup on &#96;quantity&#96;, then filter on &#96;price&#96;.[m
[32m+[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       SELECT * FROM orders_view WHERE (quantity, price) = (10, 5.00);[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
     index_usage: |[m
       Point lookup on &#96;quantity&#96;, then filter on &#96;price&#96;.[m
[32m+[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       SELECT * FROM orders_view WHERE quantity = 10 OR price = 5.00;[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
     index_usage: |[m
       Index scan. Query uses &#96;OR&#96; to combine conditions on **different** fields.[m
[32m+[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       SELECT * FROM orders_view WHERE quantity &lt;= 10;[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
     index_usage: Index scan.[m
[32m+[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       SELECT * FROM orders_view WHERE round(quantity) = 20;[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
     index_usage: Index scan.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
   - query: |[m
       &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
       -- Assume quantity is an integer[m

[31m-{{- range $data.operators -}}[m
[31m-  {{- if in .plan_types $planType -}}[m
[31m-    {{- $filteredOperators = $filteredOperators | append . -}}[m
[31m-  {{- end -}}[m
[31m-{{- end -}}[m
[31m-[m
[31m-{{- if gt (len $filteredOperators) 0 -}}[m
[31m-The following table lists the operators that are available in the {{ $planType }} plan.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- For those operators that require memory to maintain intermediate state, **Uses memory** is marked with **Yes**.[m
[31m-- For those operators that expand the data size (either rows or columns), **Can increase data size** is marked with **Yes**.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-{{- $rows := slice -}}[m
[31m-{{- range $filteredOperators -}}[m
[31m-  {{- $description := .description | markdownify -}}[m
[31m-  {{- $expansiveText := "No" -}}[m
[31m-  {{- if .expansive -}}[m
[31m-    {{- $expansiveText = .expansive_details | markdownify -}}[m
[31m-  {{- end -}}[m
[31m-  {{- $memoryText := "No" -}}[m
[31m-  {{- if .uses_memory -}}[m
[31m-    {{- $memoryText = printf "✅ %s" (.memory_details | markdownify) -}}[m
[31m-  {{- end -}}[m
[31m-  {{- $fullDescription := printf "%s\n\n**Can increase data size:** %s\n**Uses memory:** %s" $description $expansiveText $memoryText -}}[m
[31m-  {{- $row := dict "Operator" (printf "**%s**" .operator) "Description" $fullDescription "Example" (.example | markdownify) -}}[m
[31m-  {{- $rows = $rows | append $row -}}[m
[31m-{{- end -}}[m
[31m-[m
[31m-{{- $columns := slice -}}[m
[31m-{{- $columns = $columns | append (dict "column" "Operator") -}}[m
[31m-{{- $columns = $columns | append (dict "column" "Description") -}}[m
[31m-{{- $columns = $columns | append (dict "column" "Example") -}}[m
[31m-[m
[31m-{{- partial "yaml-tables/generic-table.skill.md" (dict "rows" $rows "columns" $columns) -}}[m
[31m-[m
[31m-**Notes:**[m
[31m-- **Can increase data size:** Specifies whether the operator can increase the data size (can be the number of rows or the number of columns).[m
[31m-- **Uses memory:** Specifies whether the operator use memory to maintain state for its inputs.[m
[31m-{{- else -}}[m
[31m-*No operators found for plan type "{{ $planType }}".*[m
[31m-{{- end -}}[m

[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/content/headless/index-ordering.md b/doc/user/layouts/shortcodes/index_usage/index-ordering.html[m
[33msimilarity index 58%[m
[33mrename from doc/user/content/headless/index-ordering.md[m
[33mrename to doc/user/layouts/shortcodes/index_usage/index-ordering.html[m
[33mindex 2489a2afee..5dbb88d659 100644[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/content/headless/index-ordering.md[m
[33m+++ b/doc/user/layouts/shortcodes/index_usage/index-ordering.html[m
[35m@@ -1,16 +1,12 @@[m
[31m----[m
[31m-headless: true[m
[31m----[m
[31m-[m
 Indexes in Materialize do not order their keys using the data type's natural[m
 ordering and instead orders by its internal representation of the key (the tuple[m
 of key length and value).[m
 [m
 As such, indexes in Materialize currently do not provide optimizations for:[m
 [m
[31m-- Range queries; that is queries using &#96;&gt;&#96;, &#96;&gt;=&#96;,[m
[31m-  &#96;&lt;&#96;, &#96;&lt;=&#96;, &#96;BETWEEN&#96; clauses (e.g., &#96;WHERE[m
[31m-  quantity &gt; 10&#96;,  &#96;price &gt;= 10 AND price &lt;= 50&#96;, and &#96;WHERE quantity[m
[32m+[m[32m- Range queries; that is queries using &lt;code&gt;&amp;gt;&lt;/code&gt;, &lt;code&gt;&amp;gt;=&lt;/code&gt;,[m
[32m+[m[32m  &lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;&lt;/code&gt;, &lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;=&lt;/code&gt;, &#96;BETWEEN&#96; clauses (e.g., &#96;WHERE[m
[32m+[m[32m  quantity &gt; 10&#96;,  &lt;code&gt;price &gt;= 10 AND price &lt;= 50&lt;/code&gt;, and &#96;WHERE quantity[m
   BETWEEN 10 AND 20&#96;).[m
 [m
 - &#96;GROUP BY&#96;, &#96;ORDER BY&#96; and &#96;LIMIT&#96; clauses.[m

 ## Best practices[m
[35m@@ -108,7 +117,7 @@[m [marchitecture"&gt;&lt;/p&gt;[m
 | Tier | Description |[m
 | --- | --- |[m
 | &lt;strong&gt;Source cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; | &lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;A dedicated cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; for &lt;a href="/concepts/sources/" &gt;sources&lt;/a&gt;.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;In addition, for upsert sources:&lt;/p&gt; &lt;ul&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;Consider separating upsert sources from your other sources. Upsert sources have higher resource requirements (since, for upsert sources, Materialize maintains each key and associated last value for the key as well as to perform deduplication). As such, if possible, use a separate source cluster for upsert sources.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;/li&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;Consider using a larger cluster size during snapshotting for upsert sources. Once the snapshotting operation is complete, you can downsize the cluster to align with the steady-state ingestion.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt;  |[m
[31m-| &lt;strong&gt;Compute/Transform cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; | &lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;A dedicated cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; for compute/transformation:&lt;/p&gt; &lt;ul&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;&lt;a href="/concepts/views/#materialized-views" &gt;Materialized views&lt;/a&gt; to persist, in durable storage, the results that will be served. Results of materialized views are available across all clusters.&lt;/p&gt; &gt; **Tip:** If you are using &lt;strong&gt;stacked views&lt;/strong&gt; (i.e., views whose definition depends &gt;   on other views) to reduce SQL complexity, generally, only the topmost &gt;   view (i.e., the view whose results will be served) should be a &gt;   materialized view. The underlying views that do not serve results do not &gt;   need to be materialized.  &lt;/li&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;Indexes, &lt;strong&gt;only as needed&lt;/strong&gt;, to make transformation fast (such as possibly &lt;a href="/transform-data/optimization/#optimize-multi-way-joins-with-delta-joins" &gt;indexes on join keys&lt;/a&gt;).&lt;/p&gt; &gt; **Tip:** From the compute/transformation clusters, do not create indexes on the &gt;   materialized views for the purposes of serving the view results. &gt;   Instead, use the [serving cluster(s)](#tier-serving-clusters) when &gt;   creating indexes to serve the results.  &lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt;  |[m
[32m+[m[32m| &lt;strong&gt;Compute/Transform cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; | &lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;A dedicated cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; for compute/transformation:&lt;/p&gt; &lt;ul&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;&lt;a href="/concepts/views/#materialized-views" &gt;Materialized views&lt;/a&gt; to persist, in durable storage, the results that will be served. Results of materialized views are available across all clusters.&lt;/p&gt; &gt; **Tip:** If you are using &lt;strong&gt;stacked views&lt;/strong&gt; (i.e., views whose definition depends &gt;   on other views) to reduce SQL complexity, generally, only the topmost &gt;   view (i.e., the view whose results will be served) should be a &gt;   materialized view. The underlying views that do not serve results do not &gt;   need to be materialized. &gt;     &lt;/li&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;Indexes, &lt;strong&gt;only as needed&lt;/strong&gt;, to make transformation fast (such as possibly &lt;a href="/transform-data/optimization/#optimize-multi-way-joins-with-delta-joins" &gt;indexes on join keys&lt;/a&gt;).&lt;/p&gt; &gt; **Tip:** From the compute/transformation clusters, do not create indexes on the &gt;   materialized views for the purposes of serving the view results. &gt;   Instead, use the [serving cluster(s)](#tier-serving-clusters) when &gt;   creating indexes to serve the results. &gt;  &gt;     &lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt;  |[m
 | &lt;strong&gt;Serving cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; | &lt;a name="tier-serving-clusters"&gt;&lt;/a&gt; &lt;strong&gt;A dedicated cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; for serving queries, including &lt;a href="/concepts/indexes/" &gt;indexes&lt;/a&gt; on the materialized views. Indexes are local to the cluster in which they are created. |[m
 [m
 &lt;p&gt;Benefits of a three-tier architecture include:&lt;/p&gt;[m

 ## Namespaces[m
 [m
 [m
[31m-Namespaces are a way to organize Materialize objects logically. In organizations[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Namespaces are a way to organize Materialize objects logically. In organizations[m
 with multiple objects, namespaces help avoid naming conflicts and make it easier[m
[31m-to manage objects.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-## Namespace hierarchy[m
[31m-[m
[31m-Materialize follows SQL standard's namespace hierarchy for most objects (for the[m
[31m-exceptions, see [Other objects](#other-objects)).[m
[31m-[m
[31m-|                           |             |[m
[31m-|---------------------------| ------------|[m
[31m-| 1st/Highest level:        |  **Database** |[m
[31m-| 2nd level:                |  **Schema**   |[m
[31m-| 3rd level:                | &lt;table&gt;&lt;tbody&gt;&lt;tr&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Table**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**View**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Materialized view**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Connection**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Source**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Sink**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Index**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Type**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Function**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Secret**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;/tr&gt;&lt;/tbody&gt;&lt;/table&gt;|[m
[31m-| 4th/Lowest level:             | **Column**     |[m
[31m-[m
[31m-Each layer in the hierarchy can contain elements from the level immediately[m
[31m-beneath it. That is,[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- Databases can contain: schemas;[m
[31m-- Schemas can contain: tables, views, materialized views, connections, sources,[m
[31m-sinks, indexes, types, functions, and secrets;[m
[31m-- Tables, views, and materialized views can contain: columns.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-[m
[31m-### Qualifying names[m
[31m-[m
[31m-Namespaces enable disambiguation and access to objects across different[m
[32m+[m[32mto manage objects.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h2 id="namespace-hierarchy"&gt;Namespace hierarchy&lt;/h2&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Materialize follows SQL standard&amp;rsquo;s namespace hierarchy for most objects (for the[m
[32m+[m[32mexceptions, see &lt;a href="#other-objects" &gt;Other objects&lt;/a&gt;).&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;table&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;thead&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;th&gt;&lt;/th&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;th&gt;&lt;/th&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/thead&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tbody&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;1st/Highest level:&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Database&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;2nd level:&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Schema&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;3rd level:&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;&lt;table&gt;&lt;tbody&gt;&lt;tr&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Table&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;View&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Materialized view&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Connection&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Source&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Sink&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Index&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Type&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Function&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Secret&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;/tr&gt;&lt;/tbody&gt;&lt;/table&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;4th/Lowest level:&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Column&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tbody&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/table&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Each layer in the hierarchy can contain elements from the level immediately[m
[32m+[m[32mbeneath it. That is,&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;Databases can contain: schemas;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;Schemas can contain: tables, views, materialized views, connections, sources,[m
[32m+[m[32msinks, indexes, types, functions, and secrets;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;Tables, views, and materialized views can contain: columns.&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h3 id="qualifying-names"&gt;Qualifying names&lt;/h3&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Namespaces enable disambiguation and access to objects across different[m
 databases and schemas. Namespaces use the dot notation format[m
[31m-(&#96;&lt;database&gt;.&lt;schema&gt;....&#96;) and allow you to refer to objects by:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- **Fully qualified names**[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  Used to reference objects in a different database (Materialize allows[m
[31m-  cross-database queries); e.g.,[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-  &lt;Database&gt;.&lt;Schema&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;Database&gt;.&lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;Source&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;Database&gt;.&lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;View&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;Database&gt;.&lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;Table&gt;.&lt;Column&gt;[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &gt; **Tip:** You can use fully qualified names to reference objects within the same[m
[32m+[m[32m(&lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;schema&amp;gt;....&lt;/code&gt;) and allow you to refer to objects by:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Fully qualified names&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Used to reference objects in a different database (Materialize allows[m
[32m+[m[32mcross-database queries); e.g.,&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;pre tabindex="0"&gt;&lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;Database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Source&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;View&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Table&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Column&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&gt; **Tip:** You can use fully qualified names to reference objects within the same[m
 &gt;   database (or within the same database and schema). However, for brevity and[m
 &gt;   readability, you may prefer to use qualified names instead.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Qualified names&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Used to reference objects within the same database but different schema, use[m
[32m+[m[32mthe schema and object name; e.g.,&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;pre tabindex="0"&gt;&lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Source&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;View&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Table&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Column&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Used to reference objects within the same database and schema, use the[m
[32m+[m[32mobject name; e.g.,&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;pre tabindex="0"&gt;&lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;Source&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;View&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Table&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Column&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;View&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Column&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h2 id="namespace-constraints"&gt;Namespace constraints&lt;/h2&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;All namespaces must adhere to &lt;a href="/sql/identifiers" &gt;identifier rules&lt;/a&gt;.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h2 id="other-objects"&gt;Other objects&lt;/h2&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;The following Materialize objects  exist outside the standard SQL namespace[m
[32m+[m[32mhierarchy:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Clusters&lt;/strong&gt;: Referenced directly by its name.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;For example, to create a materialized view in the cluster &lt;code&gt;cluster1&lt;/code&gt;:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;CREATE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;MATERIALIZED&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;VIEW&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;mv&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;IN&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;CLUSTER&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;cluster1&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AS&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;...&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Cluster replicas&lt;/strong&gt;: Referenced as &lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;cluster-name&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;replica-name&amp;gt;&lt;/code&gt;.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;For example, to delete replica &lt;code&gt;r1&lt;/code&gt; in cluster &lt;code&gt;cluster1&lt;/code&gt;:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;DROP&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;CLUSTER&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;REPLICA&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;cluster1&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="mf"&gt;.&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="n"&gt;r1&lt;/span&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Roles&lt;/strong&gt;: Referenced by their name. For example, to alter the &lt;code&gt;manager&lt;/code&gt; role, your SQL statement would be:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;ALTER&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;ROLE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;manager&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;...&lt;/span&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h3 id="other-object-namespace-constraints"&gt;Other object namespace constraints&lt;/h3&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Two clusters or two roles cannot have the same name. However, a cluster and a[m
[32m+[m[32mrole can have the same name.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Replicas can have the same names as long as they belong to different clusters.[m
[32m+[m[32mMaterialize automatically assigns names to replicas (e.g., &lt;code&gt;r1&lt;/code&gt;, &lt;code&gt;r2&lt;/code&gt;).&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h2 id="database-details"&gt;Database details&lt;/h2&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;By default, Materialize regions have a database named &lt;code&gt;materialize&lt;/code&gt;.&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;By default, each database has a schema called &lt;code&gt;public&lt;/code&gt;.&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;You can specify which database you connect to either when you connect (e.g.[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;code&gt;psql -d my_db ...&lt;/code&gt;) or within SQL using &lt;a href="/sql/set/" &gt;&lt;code&gt;SET DATABASE&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/a&gt; (e.g.[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;code&gt;SET DATABASE = my_db&lt;/code&gt;).&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;Materialize allows cross-database queries.&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
 [m
[31m-- **Qualified names**[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  - Used to reference objects within the same database but different schema, use[m
[31m-    the schema and object name; e.g.,[m
[31m-[m
[31m-    &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-    &lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;Source&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;View&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;Table&gt;.&lt;Column&gt;[m
[31m-    &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  - Used to reference objects within the same database and schema, use the[m
[31m-    object name; e.g.,[m
[31m-[m
[31m-    &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-    &lt;Source&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;View&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;Table&gt;.&lt;Column&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;View&gt;.&lt;Column&gt;[m
[31m-    &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-## Namespace constraints[m
[31m-[m
[31m-All namespaces must adhere to [identifier rules](/sql/identifiers).[m
[31m-[m
[31m-[m
[31m-## Other objects[m
[31m-[m
[31m-The following Materialize objects  exist outside the standard SQL namespace[m
[31m-hierarchy:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- **Clusters**: Referenced directly by its name.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  For example, to create a materialized view in the cluster &#96;cluster1&#96;:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
[31m-  CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv IN CLUSTER cluster1 AS ...;[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- **Cluster replicas**: Referenced as &#96;&lt;cluster-name&gt;.&lt;replica-name&gt;&#96;.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  For example, to delete replica &#96;r1&#96; in cluster &#96;cluster1&#96;:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
[31m-  DROP CLUSTER REPLICA cluster1.r1[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- **Roles**: Referenced by their name. For example, to alter the &#96;manager&#96; role, your SQL statement would be:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
[31m-  ALTER ROLE manager ...[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-### Other object namespace constraints[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- Two clusters or two roles cannot have the same name. However, a cluster and a[m
[31m-  role can have the same name.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- Replicas can have the same names as long as they belong to different clusters.[m
[31m-  Materialize automatically assigns names to replicas (e.g., &#96;r1&#96;, &#96;r2&#96;).[m
[31m-[m
[31m-## Database details[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- By default, Materialize regions have a database named &#96;materialize&#96;.[m
[31m-- By default, each database has a schema called &#96;public&#96;.[m
[31m-- You can specify which database you connect to either when you connect (e.g.[m
[31m-  &#96;psql -d my_db ...&#96;) or within SQL using [&#96;SET DATABASE&#96;](/sql/set/) (e.g.[m
[31m-  &#96;SET DATABASE = my_db&#96;).[m
[31m-- Materialize allows cross-database queries.[m
 [m
 [m
 ---[m
[35m@@ -199,6 +205,9 @@[m [mand serve queries.[m
 &gt; a cluster, you must increase its size.&lt;/p&gt;[m
 &gt; &lt;/li&gt;[m
 &gt; &lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 Materialize automatically assigns names to replicas (e.g., &#96;r1&#96;, &#96;r2&#96;). You can[m
[35m@@ -238,6 +247,8 @@[m [mAs your workload changes, you can [resize a cluster](/sql/alter-cluster/).[m
 [m
 &gt; **Tip:** To gauge the performance and utilization of your clusters, use the[m
 &gt; [**Environment Overview** page in the Materialize Console](/console/monitoring/).[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 ## Best practices[m
[35m@@ -256,7 +267,7 @@[m [marchitecture"&gt;&lt;/p&gt;[m
 | Tier | Description |[m
 | --- | --- |[m
 | &lt;strong&gt;Source cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; | &lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;A dedicated cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; for &lt;a href="/concepts/sources/" &gt;sources&lt;/a&gt;.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;p&gt;In addition, for upsert sources:&lt;/p&gt; &lt;ul&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;Consider separating upsert sources from your other sources. Upsert sources have higher resource requirements (since, for upsert sources, Materialize maintains each key and associated last value for the key as well as to perform deduplication). As such, if possible, use a separate source cluster for upsert sources.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;/li&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;Consider using a larger cluster size during snapshotting for upsert sources. Once the snapshotting operation is complete, you can downsize the cluster to align with the steady-state ingestion.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt;  |[m
[31m-| &lt;strong&gt;Compute/Transform cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; | &lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;A dedicated cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; for compute/transformation:&lt;/p&gt; &lt;ul&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;&lt;a href="/concepts/views/#materialized-views" &gt;Materialized views&lt;/a&gt; to persist, in durable storage, the results that will be served. Results of materialized views are available across all clusters.&lt;/p&gt; &gt; **Tip:** If you are using &lt;strong&gt;stacked views&lt;/strong&gt; (i.e., views whose definition depends &gt;   on other views) to reduce SQL complexity, generally, only the topmost &gt;   view (i.e., the view whose results will be served) should be a &gt;   materialized view. The underlying views that do not serve results do not &gt;   need to be materialized.  &lt;/li&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;Indexes, &lt;strong&gt;only as needed&lt;/strong&gt;, to make transformation fast (such as possibly &lt;a href="/transform-data/optimization/#optimize-multi-way-joins-with-delta-joins" &gt;indexes on join keys&lt;/a&gt;).&lt;/p&gt; &gt; **Tip:** From the compute/transformation clusters, do not create indexes on the &gt;   materialized views for the purposes of serving the view results. &gt;   Instead, use the [serving cluster(s)](#tier-serving-clusters) when &gt;   creating indexes to serve the results.  &lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt;  |[m
[32m+[m[32m| &lt;strong&gt;Compute/Transform cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; | &lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;A dedicated cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; for compute/transformation:&lt;/p&gt; &lt;ul&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;&lt;a href="/concepts/views/#materialized-views" &gt;Materialized views&lt;/a&gt; to persist, in durable storage, the results that will be served. Results of materialized views are available across all clusters.&lt;/p&gt; &gt; **Tip:** If you are using &lt;strong&gt;stacked views&lt;/strong&gt; (i.e., views whose definition depends &gt;   on other views) to reduce SQL complexity, generally, only the topmost &gt;   view (i.e., the view whose results will be served) should be a &gt;   materialized view. The underlying views that do not serve results do not &gt;   need to be materialized. &gt;     &lt;/li&gt; &lt;li&gt; &lt;p&gt;Indexes, &lt;strong&gt;only as needed&lt;/strong&gt;, to make transformation fast (such as possibly &lt;a href="/transform-data/optimization/#optimize-multi-way-joins-with-delta-joins" &gt;indexes on join keys&lt;/a&gt;).&lt;/p&gt; &gt; **Tip:** From the compute/transformation clusters, do not create indexes on the &gt;   materialized views for the purposes of serving the view results. &gt;   Instead, use the [serving cluster(s)](#tier-serving-clusters) when &gt;   creating indexes to serve the results. &gt;  &gt;     &lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt;  |[m
 | &lt;strong&gt;Serving cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; | &lt;a name="tier-serving-clusters"&gt;&lt;/a&gt; &lt;strong&gt;A dedicated cluster(s)&lt;/strong&gt; for serving queries, including &lt;a href="/concepts/indexes/" &gt;indexes&lt;/a&gt; on the materialized views. Indexes are local to the cluster in which they are created. |[m
 [m
 &lt;p&gt;Benefits of a three-tier architecture include:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[35m@@ -318,6 +329,7 @@[m [mviews](/concepts/views/#materialized-views).[m
 [m
 &gt; **Note:** In practice, you may find that you rarely need to index a source[m
 &gt; without performing some transformation using a view, etc.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 In Materialize, you can create indexes on a [source](/concepts/sources/) to[m
[35m@@ -370,6 +382,8 @@[m [mmaterialized views require no additional computation to keep results up-to-date.[m
 &gt; free. However, querying an indexed materialized view within the cluster where[m
 &gt; the index is created is faster since the results are served from memory rather[m
 &gt; than from storage.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 For best practices on using indexes, and understanding when to use indexed views[m
[35m@@ -401,6 +415,7 @@[m [mCREATE INDEX idx_on_my_view IN CLUSTER active_cluster ON my_view (...);[m
 ### Index usage[m
 [m
 &gt; **Important:** Indexes are local to a cluster. Queries in one cluster cannot use the indexes in another, different cluster.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 Unlike some other databases, Materialize can use an index to serve query results[m
[35m@@ -489,18 +504,105 @@[m [mThe following table shows various queries and whether Materialize performs a[m
 point lookup or an index scan.[m
 [m
 [m
[31m-| Query | Index Usage |[m
[31m-| --- | --- |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;IN&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;,&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;20&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;);&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;20&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. Query uses &lt;code&gt;OR&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on the &lt;strong&gt;same&lt;/strong&gt; field. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;5.00&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup on &lt;code&gt;quantity&lt;/code&gt;, then filter on &lt;code&gt;price&lt;/code&gt;. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;,&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;)&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;,&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;5.00&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;);&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup on &lt;code&gt;quantity&lt;/code&gt;, then filter on &lt;code&gt;price&lt;/code&gt;. |[m

 Consider that the view has an index on the &#96;quantity&#96; and &#96;price&#96; fields[m
[35m@@ -512,15 +614,82 @@[m [mCREATE INDEX idx_orders_view_qty_price on orders_view (quantity, price);[m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
 [m
 [m
[31m-| Query | Index Usage |[m
[31m-| --- | --- |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. Query does not include equality conditions on &lt;strong&gt;all&lt;/strong&gt; indexed fields. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. Query uses &lt;code&gt;OR&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;different&lt;/strong&gt; fields. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;3.00&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;);&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. Query uses &lt;code&gt;OR&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;same&lt;/strong&gt; field and &lt;code&gt;AND&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;different&lt;/strong&gt; fields. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;item&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="s1"&gt;&amp;#39;cupcake&amp;#39;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup on the index keys &lt;code&gt;quantity&lt;/code&gt; and &lt;code&gt;price&lt;/code&gt;, then filter on &lt;code&gt;item&lt;/code&gt;. |[m

 [m
 #### Limitations[m
[35m@@ -531,13 +700,14 @@[m [mof key length and value).[m
 [m
 As such, indexes in Materialize currently do not provide optimizations for:[m
 [m
[31m-- Range queries; that is queries using &#96;&gt;&#96;, &#96;&gt;=&#96;,[m
[31m-  &#96;&lt;&#96;, &#96;&lt;=&#96;, &#96;BETWEEN&#96; clauses (e.g., &#96;WHERE[m
[31m-  quantity &gt; 10&#96;,  &#96;price &gt;= 10 AND price &lt;= 50&#96;, and &#96;WHERE quantity[m
[32m+[m[32m- Range queries; that is queries using &lt;code&gt;&amp;gt;&lt;/code&gt;, &lt;code&gt;&amp;gt;=&lt;/code&gt;,[m
[32m+[m[32m  &lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;&lt;/code&gt;, &lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;=&lt;/code&gt;, &#96;BETWEEN&#96; clauses (e.g., &#96;WHERE[m
[32m+[m[32m  quantity &gt; 10&#96;,  &lt;code&gt;price &gt;= 10 AND price &amp;lt;= 50&lt;/code&gt;, and &#96;WHERE quantity[m
   BETWEEN 10 AND 20&#96;).[m
 [m
 - &#96;GROUP BY&#96;, &#96;ORDER BY&#96; and &#96;LIMIT&#96; clauses.[m
 [m
[32m+[m
 ### Indexes on views vs. materialized views[m
 [m
 In Materialize, both &lt;a href="/concepts/indexes" &gt;indexes&lt;/a&gt; on views and &lt;a href="/concepts/views/#materialized-views" &gt;materialized[m
[35m@@ -547,34 +717,34 @@[m [mthe view results in durable storage and can be accessed across clusters, indexes[m
 on views compute and store view results in memory within a &lt;strong&gt;single&lt;/strong&gt; cluster.[m
 &lt;p&gt;Some general guidelines for usage patterns include:&lt;/p&gt;[m
 &lt;table&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;thead&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;th&gt;Usage Pattern&lt;/th&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;th&gt;General Guideline&lt;/th&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;/thead&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;tbody&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View results are accessed from a single cluster only;&lt;br&gt;such as in a 1-cluster or a 2-cluster architecture.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View with an &lt;a href="/sql/create-index" &gt;index&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View used as a building block for stacked views; i.e., views not used to serve results.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View results are accessed across &lt;a href="/concepts/clusters" &gt;clusters&lt;/a&gt;;&lt;br&gt;such as in a 3-cluster architecture.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;Materialized view (in the transform cluster)&lt;br&gt;Index on the materialized view (in the serving cluster)&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m

[31m-          &lt;td&gt;Materialized view&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m

[32m+[m[32m&lt;thead&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;th&gt;Usage Pattern&lt;/th&gt;[m

[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View results are accessed from a single cluster only;&lt;br&gt;such as in a 1-cluster or a 2-cluster architecture.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View with an &lt;a href="/sql/create-index" &gt;index&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View used as a building block for stacked views; i.e., views not used to serve results.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View results are accessed across &lt;a href="/concepts/clusters" &gt;clusters&lt;/a&gt;;&lt;br&gt;such as in a 3-cluster architecture.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Materialized view (in the transform cluster)&lt;br&gt;Index on the materialized view (in the serving cluster)&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Use with a &lt;a href="/serve-results/sink/" &gt;sink&lt;/a&gt; or a &lt;a href="/sql/subscribe" &gt;&lt;code&gt;SUBSCRIBE&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/a&gt; operation&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Materialized view&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Use with &lt;a href="/transform-data/patterns/temporal-filters/" &gt;temporal filters&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Materialized view&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tbody&gt;[m
 &lt;/table&gt;[m
 &lt;p&gt;For example:&lt;/p&gt;[m
 [m
[35m@@ -621,6 +791,7 @@[m [mresults from memory.[m
 &gt; filters](/transform-data/patterns/temporal-filters/), avoid creating[m
 &gt; materialized views on a shared cluster used for both compute/transformat[m
 &gt; operations and serving queries. Use indexed views instead.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 [m
[35m@@ -644,6 +815,7 @@[m [mresults from memory.[m
 &gt; filters](/transform-data/patterns/temporal-filters/), avoid creating[m
 &gt; materialized views on a shared cluster used for both compute/transformat[m
 &gt; operations and serving queries. Use indexed views instead.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m

 ### Indexes and query optimizations[m
 [m
[35m@@ -1073,6 +1245,7 @@[m [mhaving to perform additional computation.[m
 &gt; computationally free. However, querying an indexed materialized view within the[m
 &gt; cluster associated with the index is faster since the results are served from[m
 &gt; memory rather than from storage.[m
