 # Indexes[m
[32m+[m
 Learn about indexes in Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 ## Overview[m
 [m
 In Materialize, indexes represent query results stored in memory **within a[m
[35m@@ -11,6 +15,7 @@[m [mviews](/concepts/views/#materialized-views).[m
 [m
 &gt; **Note:** In practice, you may find that you rarely need to index a source[m
 &gt; without performing some transformation using a view, etc.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 In Materialize, you can create indexes on a [source](/concepts/sources/) to[m
[35m@@ -63,6 +68,8 @@[m [mmaterialized views require no additional computation to keep results up-to-date.[m
 &gt; free. However, querying an indexed materialized view within the cluster where[m
 &gt; the index is created is faster since the results are served from memory rather[m
 &gt; than from storage.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 For best practices on using indexes, and understanding when to use indexed views[m
[35m@@ -94,6 +101,7 @@[m [mCREATE INDEX idx_on_my_view IN CLUSTER active_cluster ON my_view (...);[m
 ### Index usage[m
 [m
 &gt; **Important:** Indexes are local to a cluster. Queries in one cluster cannot use the indexes in another, different cluster.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 Unlike some other databases, Materialize can use an index to serve query results[m
[35m@@ -182,18 +190,105 @@[m [mThe following table shows various queries and whether Materialize performs a[m
 point lookup or an index scan.[m
 [m
 [m
[31m-| Query | Index Usage |[m
[31m-| --- | --- |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;IN&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;,&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;20&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;);&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;20&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. Query uses &lt;code&gt;OR&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on the &lt;strong&gt;same&lt;/strong&gt; field. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;5.00&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup on &lt;code&gt;quantity&lt;/code&gt;, then filter on &lt;code&gt;price&lt;/code&gt;. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;,&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;)&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;,&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;5.00&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;);&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup on &lt;code&gt;quantity&lt;/code&gt;, then filter on &lt;code&gt;price&lt;/code&gt;. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;5.00&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. Query uses &lt;code&gt;OR&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;different&lt;/strong&gt; fields. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;&amp;lt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;round&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;)&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;20&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="c1"&gt;-- Assume quantity is an integer &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="s1"&gt;&amp;#39;hello&amp;#39;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="o"&gt;::&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="nb"&gt;TEXT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="s1"&gt;&amp;#39;hello&amp;#39;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan, assuming &lt;code&gt;quantity&lt;/code&gt; field in &lt;code&gt;orders_view&lt;/code&gt; is an integer. In the first query, the quantity is implicitly cast to text. In the second query, the quantity is explicitly cast to text. |[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m

[35m@@ -205,15 +300,82 @@[m [mCREATE INDEX idx_orders_view_qty_price on orders_view (quantity, price);[m

[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. Query does not include equality conditions on &lt;strong&gt;all&lt;/strong&gt; indexed fields. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. Query uses &lt;code&gt;OR&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;different&lt;/strong&gt; fields. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;3.00&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;);&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup. Query uses &lt;code&gt;OR&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;same&lt;/strong&gt; field and &lt;code&gt;AND&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;different&lt;/strong&gt; fields. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;item&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="s1"&gt;&amp;#39;cupcake&amp;#39;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Point lookup on the index keys &lt;code&gt;quantity&lt;/code&gt; and &lt;code&gt;price&lt;/code&gt;, then filter on &lt;code&gt;item&lt;/code&gt;. |[m
[31m-| &lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;item&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="s1"&gt;&amp;#39;cupcake&amp;#39;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt; &lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt; | Index scan. Query uses &lt;code&gt;OR&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;different&lt;/strong&gt; fields. |[m

[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;&lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;SELECT&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;*&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;FROM&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;orders_view&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt;[m

[32m+[m[32mfields.[m

[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="p"&gt;(&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;3.00&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;);&lt;/span&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32mPoint lookup. Query uses &lt;code&gt;OR&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;same&lt;/strong&gt; field and &lt;code&gt;AND&lt;/code&gt; to combine conditions on &lt;strong&gt;different&lt;/strong&gt; fields.[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/td&gt;[m

[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;item&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="s1"&gt;&amp;#39;cupcake&amp;#39;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32mPoint lookup on the index keys &lt;code&gt;quantity&lt;/code&gt; and &lt;code&gt;price&lt;/code&gt;, then filter on[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;code&gt;item&lt;/code&gt;.[m

[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;WHERE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;quantity&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;10&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AND&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;price&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;2.50&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;OR&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;item&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="o"&gt;=&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="s1"&gt;&amp;#39;cupcake&amp;#39;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m

 [m
 #### Limitations[m
[35m@@ -224,13 +386,14 @@[m [mof key length and value).[m
 [m
 As such, indexes in Materialize currently do not provide optimizations for:[m
 [m
[31m-- Range queries; that is queries using &#96;&gt;&#96;, &#96;&gt;=&#96;,[m
[31m-  &#96;&lt;&#96;, &#96;&lt;=&#96;, &#96;BETWEEN&#96; clauses (e.g., &#96;WHERE[m
[31m-  quantity &gt; 10&#96;,  &#96;price &gt;= 10 AND price &lt;= 50&#96;, and &#96;WHERE quantity[m
[32m+[m[32m- Range queries; that is queries using &lt;code&gt;&amp;gt;&lt;/code&gt;, &lt;code&gt;&amp;gt;=&lt;/code&gt;,[m
[32m+[m[32m  &lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;&lt;/code&gt;, &lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;=&lt;/code&gt;, &#96;BETWEEN&#96; clauses (e.g., &#96;WHERE[m
[32m+[m[32m  quantity &gt; 10&#96;,  &lt;code&gt;price &gt;= 10 AND price &amp;lt;= 50&lt;/code&gt;, and &#96;WHERE quantity[m
   BETWEEN 10 AND 20&#96;).[m
 [m
 - &#96;GROUP BY&#96;, &#96;ORDER BY&#96; and &#96;LIMIT&#96; clauses.[m
 [m
[32m+[m
 ### Indexes on views vs. materialized views[m
 [m
 In Materialize, both &lt;a href="/concepts/indexes" &gt;indexes&lt;/a&gt; on views and &lt;a href="/concepts/views/#materialized-views" &gt;materialized[m
[35m@@ -240,34 +403,34 @@[m [mthe view results in durable storage and can be accessed across clusters, indexes[m
 on views compute and store view results in memory within a &lt;strong&gt;single&lt;/strong&gt; cluster.[m
 &lt;p&gt;Some general guidelines for usage patterns include:&lt;/p&gt;[m
 &lt;table&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;thead&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;th&gt;Usage Pattern&lt;/th&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;th&gt;General Guideline&lt;/th&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;/thead&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;tbody&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View results are accessed from a single cluster only;&lt;br&gt;such as in a 1-cluster or a 2-cluster architecture.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View with an &lt;a href="/sql/create-index" &gt;index&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View used as a building block for stacked views; i.e., views not used to serve results.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;View results are accessed across &lt;a href="/concepts/clusters" &gt;clusters&lt;/a&gt;;&lt;br&gt;such as in a 3-cluster architecture.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;Materialized view (in the transform cluster)&lt;br&gt;Index on the materialized view (in the serving cluster)&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;Use with a &lt;a href="/serve-results/sink/" &gt;sink&lt;/a&gt; or a &lt;a href="/sql/subscribe" &gt;&lt;code&gt;SUBSCRIBE&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/a&gt; operation&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;Materialized view&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;Use with &lt;a href="/transform-data/patterns/temporal-filters/" &gt;temporal filters&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;Materialized view&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;/tbody&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;thead&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;th&gt;Usage Pattern&lt;/th&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;th&gt;General Guideline&lt;/th&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/thead&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tbody&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View results are accessed from a single cluster only;&lt;br&gt;such as in a 1-cluster or a 2-cluster architecture.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View with an &lt;a href="/sql/create-index" &gt;index&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View used as a building block for stacked views; i.e., views not used to serve results.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;View results are accessed across &lt;a href="/concepts/clusters" &gt;clusters&lt;/a&gt;;&lt;br&gt;such as in a 3-cluster architecture.&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Materialized view (in the transform cluster)&lt;br&gt;Index on the materialized view (in the serving cluster)&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Use with a &lt;a href="/serve-results/sink/" &gt;sink&lt;/a&gt; or a &lt;a href="/sql/subscribe" &gt;&lt;code&gt;SUBSCRIBE&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/a&gt; operation&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Materialized view&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Use with &lt;a href="/transform-data/patterns/temporal-filters/" &gt;temporal filters&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;Materialized view&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tbody&gt;[m
 &lt;/table&gt;[m
 &lt;p&gt;For example:&lt;/p&gt;[m
 [m
[35m@@ -314,6 +477,7 @@[m [mresults from memory.[m
 &gt; filters](/transform-data/patterns/temporal-filters/), avoid creating[m
 &gt; materialized views on a shared cluster used for both compute/transformat[m
 &gt; operations and serving queries. Use indexed views instead.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 [m
[35m@@ -337,6 +501,7 @@[m [mresults from memory.[m
 &gt; filters](/transform-data/patterns/temporal-filters/), avoid creating[m
 &gt; materialized views on a shared cluster used for both compute/transformat[m
 &gt; operations and serving queries. Use indexed views instead.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 ### Indexes and query optimizations[m
 [m
[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/namespaces/index.md b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/namespaces/index.md[m
[33mindex fa187e4ffe..addebab70c 100644[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/namespaces/index.md[m
[33m+++ b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/namespaces/index.md[m
[35m@@ -1,119 +1,128 @@[m
 # Namespaces[m
[31m-Logical namespaces for organizing objects in Materialize.[m
[31m-Namespaces are a way to organize Materialize objects logically. In organizations[m
[31m-with multiple objects, namespaces help avoid naming conflicts and make it easier[m
[31m-to manage objects.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-## Namespace hierarchy[m
[31m-[m
[31m-Materialize follows SQL standard's namespace hierarchy for most objects (for the[m
[31m-exceptions, see [Other objects](#other-objects)).[m
[31m-[m
[31m-|                           |             |[m
[31m-|---------------------------| ------------|[m
[31m-| 1st/Highest level:        |  **Database** |[m
[31m-| 2nd level:                |  **Schema**   |[m
[31m-| 3rd level:                | &lt;table&gt;&lt;tbody&gt;&lt;tr&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Table**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**View**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Materialized view**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Connection**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Source**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Sink**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Index**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Type**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Function**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;**Secret**&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;/tr&gt;&lt;/tbody&gt;&lt;/table&gt;|[m
[31m-| 4th/Lowest level:             | **Column**     |[m
 [m
[31m-Each layer in the hierarchy can contain elements from the level immediately[m
[31m-beneath it. That is,[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- Databases can contain: schemas;[m
[31m-- Schemas can contain: tables, views, materialized views, connections, sources,[m
[31m-sinks, indexes, types, functions, and secrets;[m
[31m-- Tables, views, and materialized views can contain: columns.[m
[32m+[m[32mLogical namespaces for organizing objects in Materialize.[m
 [m
 [m
[31m-### Qualifying names[m
 [m
[31m-Namespaces enable disambiguation and access to objects across different[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Namespaces are a way to organize Materialize objects logically. In organizations[m
[32m+[m[32mwith multiple objects, namespaces help avoid naming conflicts and make it easier[m
[32m+[m[32mto manage objects.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h2 id="namespace-hierarchy"&gt;Namespace hierarchy&lt;/h2&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Materialize follows SQL standard&amp;rsquo;s namespace hierarchy for most objects (for the[m
[32m+[m[32mexceptions, see &lt;a href="#other-objects" &gt;Other objects&lt;/a&gt;).&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;table&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;thead&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;th&gt;&lt;/th&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;th&gt;&lt;/th&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/thead&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tbody&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;1st/Highest level:&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Database&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;2nd level:&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Schema&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;3rd level:&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;&lt;table&gt;&lt;tbody&gt;&lt;tr&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Table&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;View&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Materialized view&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Connection&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Source&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Sink&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Index&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;td&gt;&lt;ul&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Type&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Function&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;li&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Secret&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/li&gt;&lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;&lt;/tr&gt;&lt;/tbody&gt;&lt;/table&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;4th/Lowest level:&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Column&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tbody&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/table&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Each layer in the hierarchy can contain elements from the level immediately[m
[32m+[m[32mbeneath it. That is,&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;Databases can contain: schemas;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;Schemas can contain: tables, views, materialized views, connections, sources,[m
[32m+[m[32msinks, indexes, types, functions, and secrets;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;Tables, views, and materialized views can contain: columns.&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h3 id="qualifying-names"&gt;Qualifying names&lt;/h3&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Namespaces enable disambiguation and access to objects across different[m
 databases and schemas. Namespaces use the dot notation format[m
[31m-(&#96;&lt;database&gt;.&lt;schema&gt;....&#96;) and allow you to refer to objects by:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- **Fully qualified names**[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  Used to reference objects in a different database (Materialize allows[m
[31m-  cross-database queries); e.g.,[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-  &lt;Database&gt;.&lt;Schema&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;Database&gt;.&lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;Source&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;Database&gt;.&lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;View&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;Database&gt;.&lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;Table&gt;.&lt;Column&gt;[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &gt; **Tip:** You can use fully qualified names to reference objects within the same[m
[32m+[m[32m(&lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;schema&amp;gt;....&lt;/code&gt;) and allow you to refer to objects by:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Fully qualified names&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Used to reference objects in a different database (Materialize allows[m
[32m+[m[32mcross-database queries); e.g.,&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;pre tabindex="0"&gt;&lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;Database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Source&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;View&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Database&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Table&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Column&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&gt; **Tip:** You can use fully qualified names to reference objects within the same[m
 &gt;   database (or within the same database and schema). However, for brevity and[m
 &gt;   readability, you may prefer to use qualified names instead.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- **Qualified names**[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  - Used to reference objects within the same database but different schema, use[m
[31m-    the schema and object name; e.g.,[m
[31m-[m
[31m-    &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-    &lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;Source&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;View&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;Schema&gt;.&lt;Table&gt;.&lt;Column&gt;[m
[31m-    &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  - Used to reference objects within the same database and schema, use the[m
[31m-    object name; e.g.,[m
[31m-[m
[31m-    &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-    &lt;Source&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;View&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;Table&gt;.&lt;Column&gt;[m
[31m-    &lt;View&gt;.&lt;Column&gt;[m
[31m-    &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-## Namespace constraints[m
[31m-[m
[31m-All namespaces must adhere to [identifier rules](/sql/identifiers).[m
[31m-[m
[31m-[m
[31m-## Other objects[m
[31m-[m
[31m-The following Materialize objects  exist outside the standard SQL namespace[m
[31m-hierarchy:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- **Clusters**: Referenced directly by its name.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  For example, to create a materialized view in the cluster &#96;cluster1&#96;:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
[31m-  CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv IN CLUSTER cluster1 AS ...;[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- **Cluster replicas**: Referenced as &#96;&lt;cluster-name&gt;.&lt;replica-name&gt;&#96;.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  For example, to delete replica &#96;r1&#96; in cluster &#96;cluster1&#96;:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
[31m-  DROP CLUSTER REPLICA cluster1.r1[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- **Roles**: Referenced by their name. For example, to alter the &#96;manager&#96; role, your SQL statement would be:[m
[31m-[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
[31m-  ALTER ROLE manager ...[m
[31m-  &#96;&#96;&#96;[m
[31m-[m
[31m-### Other object namespace constraints[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- Two clusters or two roles cannot have the same name. However, a cluster and a[m
[31m-  role can have the same name.[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- Replicas can have the same names as long as they belong to different clusters.[m
[31m-  Materialize automatically assigns names to replicas (e.g., &#96;r1&#96;, &#96;r2&#96;).[m
[31m-[m
[31m-## Database details[m
[31m-[m
[31m-- By default, Materialize regions have a database named &#96;materialize&#96;.[m
[31m-- By default, each database has a schema called &#96;public&#96;.[m
[31m-- You can specify which database you connect to either when you connect (e.g.[m
[31m-  &#96;psql -d my_db ...&#96;) or within SQL using [&#96;SET DATABASE&#96;](/sql/set/) (e.g.[m
[31m-  &#96;SET DATABASE = my_db&#96;).[m
[31m-- Materialize allows cross-database queries.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Qualified names&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Used to reference objects within the same database but different schema, use[m
[32m+[m[32mthe schema and object name; e.g.,&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;pre tabindex="0"&gt;&lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Source&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;View&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Schema&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Table&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Column&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Used to reference objects within the same database and schema, use the[m
[32m+[m[32mobject name; e.g.,&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;pre tabindex="0"&gt;&lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;Source&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;View&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;Table&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Column&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&amp;lt;View&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;Column&amp;gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h2 id="namespace-constraints"&gt;Namespace constraints&lt;/h2&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;All namespaces must adhere to &lt;a href="/sql/identifiers" &gt;identifier rules&lt;/a&gt;.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h2 id="other-objects"&gt;Other objects&lt;/h2&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;The following Materialize objects  exist outside the standard SQL namespace[m
[32m+[m[32mhierarchy:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Clusters&lt;/strong&gt;: Referenced directly by its name.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;For example, to create a materialized view in the cluster &lt;code&gt;cluster1&lt;/code&gt;:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;CREATE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;MATERIALIZED&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;VIEW&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;mv&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;IN&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;CLUSTER&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;cluster1&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;AS&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;...&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="p"&gt;;&lt;/span&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Cluster replicas&lt;/strong&gt;: Referenced as &lt;code&gt;&amp;lt;cluster-name&amp;gt;.&amp;lt;replica-name&amp;gt;&lt;/code&gt;.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;For example, to delete replica &lt;code&gt;r1&lt;/code&gt; in cluster &lt;code&gt;cluster1&lt;/code&gt;:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;DROP&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;CLUSTER&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;REPLICA&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;cluster1&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="mf"&gt;.&lt;/span&gt;&lt;span class="n"&gt;r1&lt;/span&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Roles&lt;/strong&gt;: Referenced by their name. For example, to alter the &lt;code&gt;manager&lt;/code&gt; role, your SQL statement would be:&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;div class="highlight"&gt;&lt;pre tabindex="0" class="chroma"&gt;&lt;code class="language-mzsql" data-lang="mzsql"&gt;&lt;span class="line"&gt;&lt;span class="cl"&gt;&lt;span class="k"&gt;ALTER&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="k"&gt;ROLE&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="n"&gt;manager&lt;/span&gt; &lt;span class="mf"&gt;...&lt;/span&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/span&gt;&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/pre&gt;&lt;/div&gt;&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h3 id="other-object-namespace-constraints"&gt;Other object namespace constraints&lt;/h3&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Two clusters or two roles cannot have the same name. However, a cluster and a[m
[32m+[m[32mrole can have the same name.&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;p&gt;Replicas can have the same names as long as they belong to different clusters.[m
[32m+[m[32mMaterialize automatically assigns names to replicas (e.g., &lt;code&gt;r1&lt;/code&gt;, &lt;code&gt;r2&lt;/code&gt;).&lt;/p&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;h2 id="database-details"&gt;Database details&lt;/h2&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;ul&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;By default, Materialize regions have a database named &lt;code&gt;materialize&lt;/code&gt;.&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;By default, each database has a schema called &lt;code&gt;public&lt;/code&gt;.&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;You can specify which database you connect to either when you connect (e.g.[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;code&gt;psql -d my_db ...&lt;/code&gt;) or within SQL using &lt;a href="/sql/set/" &gt;&lt;code&gt;SET DATABASE&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/a&gt; (e.g.[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;code&gt;SET DATABASE = my_db&lt;/code&gt;).&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;li&gt;Materialize allows cross-database queries.&lt;/li&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/ul&gt;[m
[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/reaction-time/index.md b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/reaction-time/index.md[m
[33mindex b0e5b83f85..4e261b2552 100644[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/reaction-time/index.md[m
[33m+++ b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/reaction-time/index.md[m
[35m@@ -1,5 +1,9 @@[m
 # Reaction Time, Freshness, and Query Latency[m
[32m+[m
 Learn about indexes in Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 In operational data systems, the performance and responsiveness of queries depend not only on how fast a query runs, but also on how current the underlying data is. This page introduces three foundational concepts for evaluating and understanding system responsiveness in Materialize:[m
 [m
 * **Freshness**: the time it takes for a change in an upstream system to become visible in the results of a query.[m
[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/sinks/index.md b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/sinks/index.md[m
[33mindex 8ee09a8a3a..cedf70e941 100644[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/sinks/index.md[m
[33m+++ b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/sinks/index.md[m
[35m@@ -1,5 +1,9 @@[m
 # Sinks[m
[32m+[m
 Learn about sinks in Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 ## Overview[m
 [m
 Sinks are the inverse of sources and represent a connection to an external[m

[33mindex 1d72154aaa..b66fe09b94 100644[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/sources/index.md[m

 # Sources[m
[32m+[m
 Learn about sources in Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 ## Overview[m
 [m
 Sources describe external systems you want Materialize to read data from, and[m
[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/views/index.md b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/views/index.md[m
[33mindex 8b53638901..1987f876db 100644[m
[33m--- a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/views/index.md[m
[33m+++ b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/concepts/views/index.md[m

 # Views[m
[32m+[m
 Learn about views in Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 ## Overview[m
 [m
 Views represent queries that are saved under a name for reference. Views provide[m
[35m@@ -123,6 +127,7 @@[m [mhaving to perform additional computation.[m
 &gt; computationally free. However, querying an indexed materialized view within the[m
 &gt; cluster associated with the index is faster since the results are served from[m
 &gt; memory rather than from storage.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 [m
[35m@@ -142,34 +147,34 @@[m [mon views compute and store view results in memory within a &lt;strong&gt;single&lt;/stron[m
 [m
 &lt;p&gt;Some general guidelines for usage patterns include:&lt;/p&gt;[m
 &lt;table&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;thead&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;th&gt;Usage Pattern&lt;/th&gt;[m

See &lt;a href="/security/cloud/access-control/#roles-and-privileges" &gt;Access control control&lt;/a&gt;.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt;&lt;/td&gt;       &lt;/tr&gt;   &lt;/tbody&gt; &lt;/table&gt; &lt;blockquote&gt; &lt;p&gt;&lt;strong&gt;Note:&lt;/strong&gt; - The first user for an organization is automatically assigned the &lt;strong&gt;Organization Admin&lt;/strong&gt; role.&lt;/p&gt; &lt;ul&gt; &lt;li&gt;An &lt;a href="/security/cloud/users-service-accounts/#organization-roles" &gt;Organization Admin&lt;/a&gt; has &lt;red&gt;&lt;strong&gt;superuser&lt;/strong&gt;&lt;/red&gt; privileges in the database. Following the principle of least privilege, only assign &lt;strong&gt;Organization Admin&lt;/strong&gt; role to those users who require superuser privileges.&lt;/li&gt; &lt;li&gt;Users/service accounts can be granted additional database roles and privileges as needed.&lt;/li&gt; &lt;/ul&gt; &lt;/blockquote&gt;  |[m

 1. **For a new service account only**.[m
 [m
    For a new service account, after creating the new app password, you must[m
    connect with the service account to complete the account creation. The first time the account connects, a database role with the same name as the[m
[31m-specified service account **User** is created, and the service account creation is complete.[m
[32m+[m[32mspecified service account &lt;strong&gt;User&lt;/strong&gt; is created, and the service account creation is complete.[m
 [m

 ## Snapshotting[m
[35m@@ -112,12 +114,19 @@[m [mas it arrives, in real time.[m
 [m
 ### Modifying an existing source[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=34}}[m
 When you add a new subsource to an existing source ([&#96;ALTER SOURCE ... ADD[m
 SUBSOURCE ...&#96;](/sql/alter-source/)), Materialize starts the snapshotting[m
 process for the new subsource. During this snapshotting, the data ingestion for[m
 the existing subsources for the same source is temporarily blocked. As such, if[m
 possible, you can resize the cluster to speed up the snapshotting process and[m
 once the process finishes, resize the cluster for steady-state.[m
[32m+[m[32m{{__hugo_ctx/}}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 [m
 ## Running/steady-state[m
 [m

 &gt; See [Best practices](#best-practices) for more details.[m

[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 &gt;    For example, if the first document has &#96;price: 40&#96;, the registered Avro[m
 &gt;    schema infers the &#96;price&#96; field type to be &#96;int&#96;. If a subsequent document[m
 &gt;    has &#96;price: 2.25&#96; (a decimal value), it will cause a schema mismatch error.[m
 &gt;    To avoid this, explicitly specify the BSON type in your documents using[m
 &gt;    &#96;NumberDecimal()&#96;, e.g., &#96;price: NumberDecimal("40.00")&#96; and &#96;price:[m
 &gt;    NumberDecimal("2.25")&#96;.[m

 ### Modifying an existing source[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=34}}[m
 When you add a new subsource to an existing source ([&#96;ALTER SOURCE ... ADD[m
 SUBSOURCE ...&#96;](/sql/alter-source/)), Materialize starts the snapshotting[m
 process for the new subsource. During this snapshotting, the data ingestion for[m
 the existing subsources for the same source is temporarily blocked. As such, if[m
 possible, you can resize the cluster to speed up the snapshotting process and[m
 once the process finishes, resize the cluster for steady-state.[m
[32m+[m[32m{{__hugo_ctx/}}[m

 possible, you can resize the cluster to speed up the snapshotting process and[m

 the upstream database, the number of tables (more tables can be parallelized in Postgres), and the &lt;a href="/sql/create-cluster/#size" &gt;size of your ingestion cluster&lt;/a&gt;.&lt;/p&gt;[m
 &lt;p&gt;We&amp;rsquo;ve observed the following approximate snapshot rates from PostgreSQL:&lt;/p&gt;[m
 &lt;table&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;thead&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;th&gt;Cluster Size&lt;/th&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;th&gt;Snapshot Rate&lt;/th&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;/thead&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;tbody&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;25 cc&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;~20 MB/s&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;100 cc&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;~50 MB/s&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;tr&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;800 cc&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-          &lt;td&gt;~200 MB/s&lt;/td&gt;[m
[31m-      &lt;/tr&gt;[m
[31m-  &lt;/tbody&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;thead&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;th&gt;Cluster Size&lt;/th&gt;[m

[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;25 cc&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;~20 MB/s&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;100 cc&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;~50 MB/s&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;tr&gt;[m

[32m+[m[32m&lt;td&gt;~200 MB/s&lt;/td&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&lt;/tr&gt;[m

 &gt; Materialize to use the &#96;UPSERT&#96; envelope.[m

[35m@@ -190,13 +192,16 @@[m [mRegister the MongoDB connector with the following configuration:[m
 &gt;    different BSON types across documents, ensure that same field uses a[m
 &gt;    consistent BSON type across the collection to avoid schema inference[m
 &gt;    errors.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 &gt;    For example, if the first document has &#96;price: 40&#96;, the registered Avro[m
 &gt;    schema infers the &#96;price&#96; field type to be &#96;int&#96;. If a subsequent document[m
 &gt;    has &#96;price: 2.25&#96; (a decimal value), it will cause a schema mismatch error.[m
 &gt;    To avoid this, explicitly specify the BSON type in your documents using[m
 &gt;    &#96;NumberDecimal()&#96;, e.g., &#96;price: NumberDecimal("40.00")&#96; and &#96;price:[m
 &gt;    NumberDecimal("2.25")&#96;.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 &gt;    If you cannot enforce a consistent BSON type, you can omit the unwrap.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 2. Register the connector with Kafka Connect:[m

 ## Considerations[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=36}}[m
 ### Schema changes[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=37}}[m
 &gt; **Note:** Work to more smoothly support ddl changes to upstream tables is currently in[m
 &gt; progress. The work introduces the ability to re-ingest the same upstream table[m
 &gt; under a new schema and switch over without downtime.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m{{__hugo_ctx/}}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 [m
 Materialize supports schema changes in the upstream database as follows:[m

 ### Modifying an existing source[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=34}}[m
 When you add a new subsource to an existing source ([&#96;ALTER SOURCE ... ADD[m
 SUBSOURCE ...&#96;](/sql/alter-source/)), Materialize starts the snapshotting[m
 process for the new subsource. During this snapshotting, the data ingestion for[m
 the existing subsources for the same source is temporarily blocked. As such, if[m
 possible, you can resize the cluster to speed up the snapshotting process and[m
 once the process finishes, resize the cluster for steady-state.[m
[32m+[m[32m{{__hugo_ctx/}}[m

 # Ingest data from Amazon RDS[m
[32m+[m
 How to stream data from Amazon RDS for MySQL to Materialize[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 This page shows you how to stream data from [Amazon RDS for MySQL](https://aws.amazon.com/rds/mysql/)[m
 to Materialize using the [MySQL source](/sql/create-source/mysql).[m
 [m
 &gt; **Tip:** For help getting started with your own data, you can schedule a [free guided[m
 &gt; trial](https://materialize.com/demo/?utm_campaign=General&amp;utm_source=documentation).[m

[35m@@ -1,10 +1,16 @@[m
 # Ingest data from Google Cloud SQL[m
[32m+[m
 How to stream data from Google Cloud SQL for MySQL to Materialize[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 This page shows you how to stream data from [Google Cloud SQL for MySQL](https://cloud.google.com/sql/MySQL)[m
 to Materialize using the[MySQL source](/sql/create-source/mysql/).[m
 [m
 &gt; **Tip:** For help getting started with your own data, you can schedule a [free guided[m
 &gt; trial](https://materialize.com/demo/?utm_campaign=General&amp;utm_source=documentation).[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 ## Before you begin[m
[35m@@ -434,6 +440,7 @@[m [muser for Materialize with sufficient privileges to manage replication.[m
 &gt; **Note:** If you are prototyping and your Google Cloud SQL instance is publicly[m
 &gt; accessible, **you can skip this step**. For production scenarios, we recommend[m
 &gt; configuring one of the network security options below.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 [m
[35m@@ -576,6 +583,7 @@[m [mbastion host.[m
 &gt; source (e.g. &#96;quickstart&#96;), **you can skip this step**. For production[m
 &gt; scenarios, we recommend separating your workloads into multiple clusters for[m
 &gt; [resource isolation](/sql/create-cluster/#resource-isolation).[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 In Materialize, a [cluster](/concepts/clusters/) is an isolated[m
[35m@@ -716,6 +724,8 @@[m [myour networking configuration.[m
 Once you have created the connection, you can use the connection in the[m
 [&#96;CREATE SOURCE&#96;](/sql/create-source/) command to connect to your MySQL instance and start ingesting[m
 data:[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 &#96;&#96;&#96;mzsql[m
 CREATE SOURCE mz_source[m
   FROM MYSQL CONNECTION mysql_connection[m

 [m
 ### Modifying an existing source[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=34}}[m
 When you add a new subsource to an existing source ([&#96;ALTER SOURCE ... ADD[m
 SUBSOURCE ...&#96;](/sql/alter-source/)), Materialize starts the snapshotting[m
 process for the new subsource. During this snapshotting, the data ingestion for[m
 the existing subsources for the same source is temporarily blocked. As such, if[m
 possible, you can resize the cluster to speed up the snapshotting process and[m
 once the process finishes, resize the cluster for steady-state.[m
[32m+[m[32m{{__hugo_ctx/}}[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m{{__hugo_ctx/}}[m

[33m+++ b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/ingest-data/mysql/index.md[m
[35m@@ -31,6 +31,7 @@[m [mgives you the following benefits:[m
 &gt; **Note:** MySQL-compatible database systems are not guaranteed to work with the MySQL[m
 &gt; source out-of-the-box. [MariaDB](https://mariadb.org/), [Vitess](https://vitess.io/)[m
 &gt; and [PlanetScale](https://planetscale.com/) are currently **not supported**.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 The MySQL source requires **MySQL 5.7+** and is compatible with most common[m

 ### Modifying an existing source[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=34}}[m
 When you add a new subsource to an existing source ([&#96;ALTER SOURCE ... ADD[m
 SUBSOURCE ...&#96;](/sql/alter-source/)), Materialize starts the snapshotting[m
 process for the new subsource. During this snapshotting, the data ingestion for[m
 the existing subsources for the same source is temporarily blocked. As such, if[m
 possible, you can resize the cluster to speed up the snapshotting process and[m
 once the process finishes, resize the cluster for steady-state.[m
[32m+[m[32m{{__hugo_ctx/}}[m

 ### Modifying an existing source[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=34}}[m
 When you add a new subsource to an existing source ([&#96;ALTER SOURCE ... ADD[m
 SUBSOURCE ...&#96;](/sql/alter-source/)), Materialize starts the snapshotting[m
 process for the new subsource. During this snapshotting, the data ingestion for[m
 the existing subsources for the same source is temporarily blocked. As such, if[m
 possible, you can resize the cluster to speed up the snapshotting process and[m
 once the process finishes, resize the cluster for steady-state.[m
[32m+[m[32m{{__hugo_ctx/}}[m

 # AWS PrivateLink connections (Cloud-only)[m
[32m+[m
 How to connect Materialize Cloud to a Kafka broker, a Confluent Schema Registry server, a PostgreSQL database, or a MySQL database through an AWS PrivateLink service.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 Materialize can connect to a Kafka broker, a Confluent Schema Registry server, a[m
 PostgreSQL database, or a MySQL database through an [AWS PrivateLink](https://aws.amazon.com/privatelink/)[m

[32m+[m
 How to connect Materialize to a Kafka broker, a MySQL database, or PostgreSQL database using an SSH tunnel connection to a SSH bastion server[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 **Cloud:**[m
 Materialize can connect to a Kafka broker, a Confluent Schema Registry server, a[m
 PostgreSQL database, or a MySQL database through an SSH tunnel connection. In[m
[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/ingest-data/network-security/static-ips/index.md b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/ingest-data/network-security/static-ips/index.md[m

[35m@@ -1,5 +1,9 @@[m
 # Static IP addresses (Cloud-only)[m
[32m+[m
 Materialize Cloud provides static IP addresses that you can use to configure egress policies in your virtual networks that target outbound traffic to Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 Each Materialize Cloud region is associated with a unique set of static egress[m
 [Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR)](https://aws.amazon.com/what-is/cidr/)[m
 blocks. All connections to the public internet initiated by your Materialize[m
[35m@@ -7,6 +11,7 @@[m [mregion will originate from an IP address in the provided blocks.[m
 [m
 &gt; **Note:** On rare occasion, we may need to change the static egress CIDR blocks associated[m
 &gt; with a region. We make every effort to provide advance notice of such changes.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 When connecting Materialize to services in your private networks (e.g., Kafka,[m

 &gt; **Warning:** There is a known issue with Aurora PostgreSQL 16.1 that can cause logical replication to fail with the following error:[m
 &gt; - &#96;postgres: sql client error: db error: ERROR: could not map filenumber "base/16402/3147867235" to relation OID&#96;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 &gt; This is due to a bug in Aurora's implementation of logical replication in PostgreSQL 16.1, where the system fails to correctly fetch relation metadata from the catalogs. If you encounter these errors, you should upgrade your Aurora PostgreSQL instance to a newer minor version (16.2 or later).[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 &gt; For more information, see [this AWS discussion](https://repost.aws/questions/QU4RXUrLNQS_2oSwV34pmwww/error-could-not-map-filenumber-after-aurora-upgrade-to-16-1).[m

[32m+[m
 How to stream data from Azure DB for PostgreSQL to Materialize[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 This page shows you how to stream data from [Azure DB for PostgreSQL](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/products/postgresql)[m
 to Materialize using the [PostgreSQL source](/sql/create-source/postgres/).[m
 [m
 &gt; **Tip:** For help getting started with your own data, you can schedule a [free guided[m
 &gt; trial](https://materialize.com/demo/?utm_campaign=General&amp;utm_source=documentation).[m

 # Troubleshooting: Connection closed[m
[32m+[m
 How to troubleshoot and resolve the connection closed error with PostgreSQL sources in Materialize[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 This guide helps you troubleshoot and resolve the "connection closed" error that[m
 can occur with PostgreSQL sources in Materialize.[m
 [m
[35m@@ -18,6 +22,7 @@[m [mreplicate data from PostgreSQL was closed, interrupting the replication process.[m
 &gt; **Note:** This error is known to occur during Materialize maintenance windows and can be[m
 &gt; safely ignored if that is the case. Sources will automatically reconnect after[m
 &gt; maintenance is complete.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m

[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m

 &gt; **Warning:** There is a known issue with Aurora PostgreSQL 16.1 that can cause logical replication to fail with the following error:[m
 &gt; - &#96;postgres: sql client error: db error: ERROR: could not map filenumber "base/16402/3147867235" to relation OID&#96;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 &gt; This is due to a bug in Aurora's implementation of logical replication in PostgreSQL 16.1, where the system fails to correctly fetch relation metadata from the catalogs. If you encounter these errors, you should upgrade your Aurora PostgreSQL instance to a newer minor version (16.2 or later).[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 &gt; For more information, see [this AWS discussion](https://repost.aws/questions/QU4RXUrLNQS_2oSwV34pmwww/error-could-not-map-filenumber-after-aurora-upgrade-to-16-1).[m

 ### Modifying an existing source[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=34}}[m
 When you add a new subsource to an existing source ([&#96;ALTER SOURCE ... ADD[m
 SUBSOURCE ...&#96;](/sql/alter-source/)), Materialize starts the snapshotting[m
 process for the new subsource. During this snapshotting, the data ingestion for[m
 the existing subsources for the same source is temporarily blocked. As such, if[m
 possible, you can resize the cluster to speed up the snapshotting process and[m
 once the process finishes, resize the cluster for steady-state.[m

 # Ingest data from self-hosted SQL Server[m
[32m+[m
 How to stream data from self-hosted SQL Server database to Materialize[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 This page shows you how to stream data from a self-hosted SQL Server database[m
 to Materialize using the [SQL Server Source](/sql/create-source/sql-server/).[m
 [m
 &gt; **Tip:** For help getting started with your own data, you can schedule a [free guided[m
 &gt; trial](https://materialize.com/demo/?utm_campaign=General&amp;utm_source=documentation).[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 ## Before you begin[m
[35m@@ -34,6 +40,8 @@[m [mwhich is not readily available on older versions of SQL Server.[m
 &gt; **Note:** To configure SQL Server for data ingestion into Materialize, you must be a user[m
 &gt; with privileges to enable CDC and create/manage login, users, roles, and[m
 &gt; privileges.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m

 ### Modifying an existing source[m
 [m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m  {{__hugo_ctx pid=34}}[m
 When you add a new subsource to an existing source ([&#96;ALTER SOURCE ... ADD[m
 SUBSOURCE ...&#96;](/sql/alter-source/)), Materialize starts the snapshotting[m
 process for the new subsource. During this snapshotting, the data ingestion for[m
 the existing subsources for the same source is temporarily blocked. As such, if[m
 possible, you can resize the cluster to speed up the snapshotting process and[m
 once the process finishes, resize the cluster for steady-state.[m
[32m+[m[32m{{__hugo_ctx/}}[m

[35m@@ -1,5 +1,9 @@[m
 # Golang cheatsheet[m
[32m+[m
 Use Go to connect, insert, manage, query and stream from Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 Materialize is **wire-compatible** with PostgreSQL, which means that Go applications can use the standard library's [&#96;database/sql&#96;](https://pkg.go.dev/database/sql) package with a PostgreSQL driver to interact with Materialize. In this guide, we'll use the [&#96;pgx&#96; driver](https://github.com/jackc/pgx) to connect to Materialize and issue SQL commands.[m
 [m
 ## Connect[m
[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/integrations/client-libraries/index.md b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/integrations/client-libraries/index.md[m

[33m+++ b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/integrations/client-libraries/index.md[m
[35m@@ -9,6 +9,7 @@[m [mlibraries to interact with Materialize and **create relations**, **execute[m
 queries** and **stream out results**.[m
 [m
 &gt; **Note:** Client libraries tend to run complex introspection queries that may use configuration settings, system tables or features not yet implemented in Materialize. This means that even if PostgreSQL is supported, it's **not guaranteed** that the same integration will work out-of-the-box.[m
[32m+[m[32m&gt;[m
 [m
 [m
 | Language | Tested drivers                                                  | Notes                                                 |[m

[32m+[m

[32m+[m
 Materialize is **wire-compatible** with PostgreSQL, which means that Java[m
 applications can use common PostgreSQL clients to interact with Materialize.[m
 This guide uses the [PostgreSQL JDBC Driver](https://jdbc.postgresql.org/) to[m

 # Node.js cheatsheet[m
[32m+[m
 Use Node.js to connect, insert, manage, query and stream from Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 Materialize is **wire-compatible** with PostgreSQL, which means that Node.js applications can use common PostgreSQL clients to interact with Materialize. In this guide, we'll use the  [&#96;node-postgres&#96; library](https://node-postgres.com/) to connect to Materialize and issue SQL commands.[m
 [m
 ## Connect[m
[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/integrations/client-libraries/php/index.md b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/integrations/client-libraries/php/index.md[m

[35m@@ -1,5 +1,9 @@[m
 # PHP cheatsheet[m
[32m+[m
 Use PHP PDO to connect, insert, manage, query and stream from Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 Materialize is **wire-compatible** with PostgreSQL, which means that PHP applications can use common PostgreSQL clients to interact with Materialize. In this guide, we'll use the [PDO_PGSQL driver](https://www.php.net/manual/en/ref.pdo-pgsql.php) to connect to Materialize and issue SQL commands.[m
 [m
 ## Connect[m

 # Python cheatsheet[m
[32m+[m
 Use Python to connect, insert, manage, query and stream from Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 Materialize is **wire-compatible** with PostgreSQL, which means that Python applications can use common PostgreSQL clients to interact with Materialize. In this guide, we'll use the [&#96;psycopg2&#96;](https://pypi.org/project/psycopg2/) adapter to connect to Materialize and issue SQL commands.[m
 [m
 ## Connect[m

 # Ruby cheatsheet[m
[32m+[m
 Use Ruby to connect, insert, manage, query and stream from Materialize.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
 Materialize is **wire-compatible** with PostgreSQL, which means that Ruby applications can use common PostgreSQL clients to interact with Materialize. In this guide, we'll use the  [&#96;pg&#96; gem](https://rubygems.org/gems/pg/) to connect to Materialize and issue SQL commands.[m
 [m
 ## Connect[m
[33mdiff --git a/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/integrations/client-libraries/rust/index.md b/doc/user/static/markdown-docs/integrations/client-libraries/rust/index.md[m
